title: How to Score Sourdough for a Spiral Design Using a Lame
meta_description: Master the art of sourdough spiral scoring with our step‑by‑step guide. Learn techniques, tools, and tips for perfect designs using a lame and our 3‑in‑1 Sourdo
html:
Scoring a sourdough loaf isn’t just about letting steam escape – it’s an opportunity to turn a simple loaf into a work of art. If you’ve ever wanted a clean, winding spiral on the crust, this sourdough spiral scoring guide will walk you through every detail, from dough preparation to the final slash with a lame. The right tools make a huge difference, and the 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set (whisk, scraper, and bread scoring lame) is a solid starter kit for beginners who want professional‑look results.

Why a Spiral? The Aesthetic and Functional Benefits

A spiral pattern not only looks impressive on a bakery shelf, it also guides the expansion of the loaf in a controlled way. The alternating cuts allow steam to escape evenly, reducing the chance of large cracks or uneven oven spring. For home bakers, a well‑executed spiral signals confidence and gives a visual cue that the dough was handled with care.

Preparing the Dough for Scoring

Before you even reach for a lame, the dough needs to be ready for a clean cut. Follow these steps:

  1. Bulk Fermentation: Let your sourdough rise until it has increased by about 30‑40% and shows bubbles on the surface. This is when the gluten network is strong enough to hold a pattern.
  2. Shape the Loaf: After the first rise, turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface. Using the dough scraper from the Sourdough Whisk Set, gently tighten the surface tension by pulling the dough toward you while rotating the bowl.
  3. Bench Rest: Let the shaped loaf rest for 20‑30 minutes. This relaxes the gluten just enough to make a smooth cut without tearing.
  4. Final Proof: Place the loaf in a banneton or on a parchment sheet and let it proof until it passes the “poke test” – the dough should slowly spring back when gently pressed.

When the dough is ready, it will feel slightly tacky but not sticky, and the surface will be smooth and slightly domed – perfect for a spiraled slash.

Choosing and Using the Right Lame

The bread scoring lame is the tool that creates the design. A good lame has a razor‑sharp blade and a comfortable handle for precise control. The 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set includes a high‑quality lame that balances weight and agility, making it ideal for beginners learning to execute a spiral.

Here’s how to wield it effectively:

  • Sharpness Check: Run the blade lightly over a piece of parchment. It should glide without tearing. If it drags, use a honing stone or replace the blade.
  • Angle: Hold the lame at a 30‑45° angle to the dough surface. This angle encourages the cut to open as the loaf expands.
  • Speed: A quick, confident motion is key. Slow, hesitant strokes cause the dough to compress and result in ragged edges.

Practice on a small piece of dough before scoring your final loaf. This builds muscle memory and helps you gauge the pressure needed for a clean spiral.

Creating the Spiral Design Step‑by‑Step

Now for the fun part – the scoring itself. Follow this method to achieve a tight, even spiral:

  1. Mark the Center: Lightly press the tip of the lame into the center of the loaf – this is where the spiral will begin.
  2. First Arc: Sweep the blade outward in a half‑circle, maintaining the same angle. Aim for a radius of about 1‑1.5 inches.
  3. Second Arc: Return the blade to the starting point and swing outward again, this time slightly larger than the first arc. Continue expanding each successive arc, keeping the motion smooth.
  4. Finish the Loop: When you reach the edge of the loaf, stop the blade and lift it cleanly. The cuts should be continuous, resembling a snail’s shell.

Key tips:

  • Keep the blade perpendicular to the surface when changing direction to avoid jagged edges.
  • Maintain even pressure throughout the motion; too much can tear the dough, too little will not cut through the crust.
  • If the dough is overly sticky, lightly dust the lame with flour to reduce friction.

Baking and Finishing the Spiral

Slide the scored loaf onto a preheated baking stone or a heavy-duty sheet pan. Bake according to your recipe, usually starting with a high temperature (475°F) for the first 15 minutes, then lowering to 425°F for the remainder. The steam generated from the cuts helps the spiral open dramatically, creating a crisp, decorative pattern.

When the crust reaches a deep golden brown and the internal temperature hits 205°F, remove the loaf. Let it cool on a rack for at least an hour before slicing – this allows the crumb to set and the spiral to retain its shape.

Cleaning and Maintaining Your Lame

After you’ve impressed friends with a beautiful sourdough spiral, take a moment to care for your scoring tool. Rinse the blade with warm water, wipe it dry, and store it in a protective sheath (often included with the 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set). Avoid abrasive cleaners; a simple wipe is enough to keep the edge razor‑sharp for future bakes.

FAQ

Do I need a special lame for a spiral, or will any razor work?

Any razor‑sharp blade can create a spiral, but a dedicated bread scoring lame offers better balance and a comfortable grip, making precise, repeated motions easier – especially for beginners.

Can I use the spiral scoring technique on whole‑grain or rye doughs?

Yes, but denser doughs require a slightly sharper blade and a slower motion. The crust may not open as dramatically, but the design will still be visible.

How often should I replace the blade on my lame?

When the blade starts to feel dull or pulls at the dough rather than cutting cleanly, it’s time to replace it. With regular honing, a good quality lame can last many loaves.
